author: jdegoes
body: I would think about adding `schema-iron` that derives `Schema[A :| B]` assuming `Schema[A]` exists, and does that by using `.transform` on the schema, ensuring runtime validation.

author: EstebanMarin
body:
@jdegoes I played with the concept https://github.com/zio/zio-blocks/pull/1097 I added schema-iron and Kind of worked but there was impedance through the way both libraries handle opaque types. 

Instead of using Iron should there be a zio-block interoperable refined type?

author: jdegoes
body: I think this does fit, but the question is before or after Concurrent. They will be easier to implement afterwards.

author: jdegoes
body:
An item in a Resource pool is defined by:

 - the real Resource
 - a 'recycle' function to prepare the real resource for reuse (`def recycle`).

This is probably `SharedResource`. 

Then a Pool could accept either Resource, which means to allocate a new resource each time, or SharedResource, which means existing resources can be reused when they are done.

Then the fundamental thing one can do with a pool is use something inside the pool (`lease`), which should of course return an ordinary Resource.

```scala
Scope.global.scoped { scope =>
  import scope.*

  val pool: Pool[Connection] @@ scope.Tag = allocate(Pool.shared[Connection])

  val lease: Resource[Connection] @@ scope.Tag = $(pool)(_.pool.lease)

  val connection: Connection @@ scope.Tag = allocate(lease)
}
```

author: ghostdogpr
body: It's strange because `equals` returns true and they have the same hashCode so they seem equivalent, but instance overrides by TypeId don't seem to work. I'll dig deeper to find why.

author: ghostdogpr
body: Ah they're equivalent if you compare them directly but not when they're used as type params of a collection.

author: ghostdogpr
body:
With List:
```scala
import zio.blocks.schema.*
import zio.blocks.typeid.*

object Types {
  opaque type Id[+A] = Int
}

trait Foo

type FooId = Types.Id[Foo]

val t1 = TypeId.of[List[FooId]]
println(t1)

case class A(id: List[FooId]) derives Schema

val t2 = Schema[A].reflect.asRecord.get.fields.head.value.typeId
println(t2)

println(t1 == t2)
println(t1.hashCode)
println(t2.hashCode)
```
prints
```
List[FooId]
List[Id[Foo]]
true
-777338659
649582773
```
So first, why do we get a different `TypeId` from the Schema and from direct `TypeId` derivation? Then if they are completely equivalent, why do we get different `hashCode` inside a `List`?

author: 987Nabil
body:
We do some logic of structural equality. Probably that is not correctly applied to the type param.
I will take a look into it.

author: ghostdogpr
body:
Another broken case:
```scala
case class A()
case class Test(a: Option[A]) derives Schema

val t = TypeId.of[Option[A]]
println(t.toString)

val s = Schema[Test]
val t2 = s.reflect.asRecord.get.fields.head.value.typeId
println(t2.toString)
```
We get:
```
Option[A]
Option[Intersection]
```
